**Research justification (from the Meisl et al. nucleation-kinetics framework, *Nat. Protoc.* 2016 / Cohen et al. *PNAS* 2013):** The secondary-nucleation reaction flux in Aβ42 aggregation is *not* a pure power law in monomer — it **saturates**. The rate takes a Michaelis–Menten-like form because secondary nucleation proceeds via monomer adsorption onto the fibril surface: the surface has finite catalytic-site capacity, so at high monomer the nucleation flux plateaus rather than growing without bound. The canonical form is

  secondary nucleation rate ∝ k₂ · M · mⁿ² / (1 + m^n²/K_M)

i.e. the effective specific multiplication rate has a **finite ceiling** set by surface saturation. This is exactly the structural principle behind the breakt
